text.title: Behavior-Triggered Onboarding Emails Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Behavior-Triggered Onboarding Emails application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Each email in the sequence MUST fire on a tracked user behavior (action OR inaction), not on time alone. Pure day-N drips are rejected.
rule.rationale: Behavior triggers outperform time triggers by 15-40% on activation rate.

rule.statement: Each email MUST carry exactly one CTA matching the next activation step. Multi-CTA onboarding is rejected.
rule.rationale: Confused users do not activate; one ask per email holds focus.

rule.statement: Sequence MUST include an escalation ladder: nudge → help → human-offered for users stuck at the same step ≥3 times.
rule.rationale: Stuck users churn silently; escalation surfaces them before churn.

rule.statement: Sequence MUST name a single activation event the whole ladder targets. Without it, success cannot be measured.
rule.rationale: Without a named event, retention attribution collapses and the sequence cannot be optimised.

rule.statement: Sequence MUST suppress remaining emails immediately on activation. Continuing the drip post-activation is rejected.
rule.rationale: Post-activation drips read as spam and depress long-term engagement.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
